g.data-narration: Welcome to Data Modeling Concepts - the foundation of database design and modern information systems.
Data modeling is the process of creating a structured representation of data to organize, store, and retrieve information efficiently in databases.
g.text: A data model is a collection of concepts that describe the
structure of a database, providing abstraction between users
and physical storage for efficient data management.

g.data-narration: Data models serve as communication bridges between stakeholders, ensuring consistency, eliminating redundancy, and providing database blueprints.
g.text: • Communication bridge between business analysts, developers, and users
• Ensures data consistency and eliminates redundancy
• Provides clear blueprint for database implementation

g.data-narration: Data models exist at three levels: conceptual (high-level business view), logical (DBMS-specific structure), and physical (storage details).
Schema is the structural blueprint (static design), while instance is actual data stored at a moment (dynamic, changes frequently).
g.text: Actual data at a moment - Dynamic
Rows of data: insert, update, delete

g.data-narration: Three-schema architecture has external (user views), conceptual (logical structure), and internal (physical storage) levels.
Data independence allows schema changes at one level without affecting higher levels: physical (storage changes) and logical (structure changes).
g.text: Change storage without affecting logical schema
Change logical schema without affecting views
e.g., Adding new field to table

g.data-narration: The ER model (by Peter Chen, 1976) represents databases graphically with entities (rectangles), attributes (ovals), and relationships (diamonds).
g.text: Proposed by Peter Chen in 1976 - Conceptual model using graphical representations

g.data-narration: Attributes include simple (atomic), composite (divisible), single-valued, multi-valued, and derived (computed) types.
The relational model organizes data into tables with rows (tuples) and columns (attributes), using keys for unique identification.
g.text: E.F. Codd, 1970 - Data as relations (tables) with rows and columns
• Primary Key: Selected candidate (unique, NOT NULL)

g.data-narration: Keys include alternate keys (unused candidate keys), foreign keys (references to other tables), and composite keys (multi-attribute).
Cardinality ratios define relationships: 1:1 (one-to-one), 1:N (one-to-many), M:N (many-to-many) between entities.
g.text: Specifies how many instances of one entity associate with another entity
Each entity links to exactly one other
One entity links to many others
Many entities link to many others

g.data-narration: Participation constraints include total participation (every entity must participate, double-line) and partial participation (optional, single-line).
g.text: Every entity MUST participate in relationship
Represented by double line in ER diagram
Entity MAY or MAY NOT participate
Represented by single line in ER diagram

g.data-narration: Strong entities have their own primary key; weak entities depend on strong entities and use partial key combined with owner's key.
g.text: Has its own primary key - can be uniquely identified
Depends on strong entity - uses partial key

g.data-narration: To convert ER to relational: create tables for each entity, add foreign keys for relationships. For 1:N, add FK to the N-side entity.
